content.text: A debt register with 30 open items is only useful if you know which 3 to fix first. Sorting by severity score alone is not sufficient — a high-severity item that affects only a deprecated internal tool is less urgent than a medium-severity item that blocks three customer-facing features. The
Debt Impact Prioritization Framework

combines RICE scoring (borrowed from product management) with blast radius estimation and dependency mapping to produce a composite priority score that reflects both technical severity and business impact.
What This Framework Is
The framework applies a three-layer prioritization to each debt item in the register:
RICE score:
measures the business impact of leaving the debt unresolved (how many users are affected, how severely, and how certain we are, divided by effort).
Blast radius:
measures how many downstream systems, features, or teams are affected if the debt item is left unresolved or if it causes a failure.
Dependency mapping:
identifies which debt items must be resolved before others can be resolved, and which feature work is blocked by unresolved debt.
The composite score = RICE × blast_radius_multiplier. This single number can be sorted to produce a priority-ordered list that the team uses directly in sprint planning.
Why It Matters
Not all debt is equal:

a hardcoded model version that affects the entire product is 100× more urgent than a duplicated prompt string in a rarely-used feature. Without explicit scoring, teams default to FIFO ordering or whoever shouts loudest.
Blast radius multiplies impact:

a debt item that blocks three downstream features is not just high-severity for itself — it blocks the business value of everything that depends on it. This multiplicative effect is invisible to a simple severity-only sort.
Composite scoring enables principled tradeoffs:
when the team has to choose between resolving a high-RICE low-blast item and a medium-RICE high-blast item, the composite score provides a defensible answer that can be communicated to stakeholders.
Architecture: RICE + Blast Radius

attrs.alt: RICE scoring formula, blast radius dependency graph, and composite score worked examples for three debt items

content.text: RICE Scoring Applied to Technical Debt
RICE was designed for product feature prioritization, but it maps cleanly to debt items by redefining each variable in the context of "what happens if this debt is left unresolved".
Variable
Definition for Debt
How to measure
Reach (R)
Number of users affected per sprint if the debt item degrades or fails
MAU of the affected feature; 0 if only internal tooling
Impact (I)
Severity of the user-visible degradation if the debt item manifests
Scale: 0.25=negligible, 0.5=minor, 1=medium, 2=high, 3=massive (total outage)
Confidence (C)
Certainty that the estimated Reach and Impact are correct
50% = low (hypothesis), 80% = medium (data-supported), 100% = high (observed failure)
Effort (E)
Story points to resolve the debt item
Standard Fibonacci scale (1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 21)
A high Confidence score for a debt item means you have
observed
the failure in production — which means it's no longer a theoretical risk. Use 100% confidence only for debt items where the impact has already materialized in a support ticket or incident.
Blast Radius Estimation
Blast radius is the number of downstream systems, features, debt items, and teams that are affected if the debt item is left unresolved. It is measured by
counting the outbound edges
in the dependency graph rooted at the debt item.
Blast Radius Level
Downstream Nodes
Multiplier
Example
Low
Duplicated prompt string in one service, no dependents
Medium
Missing retry logic on a tool used by 2 features
High
Sync tool calls in shared client library affecting 3 features
Critical
Pinned model version powering all AI features + blocked upgrade path for downstream teams
Dependency Mapping with a Dependency Graph

Build the dependency graph by asking two questions for each debt item: (1) what features break if this debt item causes a failure? (2) what other debt items cannot be resolved until this one is? The answers are the outbound edges in the graph.

# dependency_graph.py — build and analyze the debt dependency graph
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class DebtNode:
    id: str
    title: str
    category: str
    reach: int                 # users per sprint
    impact: float              # 0.25 | 0.5 | 1 | 2 | 3
    confidence: float          # 0.0 – 1.0
    effort: int                # story points
    dependents: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)  # IDs of downstream items/features
    blocks: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)      # debt items this blocks

    @property
    def rice_score(self) -> float:
        return round((self.reach * self.impact * self.confidence) / max(self.effort, 1), 1)

    @property
    def blast_radius_level(self) -> str:
        n = len(self.dependents)
        if n == 0 or n == 1:
            return "low"
        elif n == 2:
            return "medium"
        elif n <= 4:
            return "high"
        else:
            return "critical"

    @property
    def blast_multiplier(self) -> int:
        return {"low": 1, "medium": 2, "high": 4, "critical": 5}[self.blast_radius_level]

    @property
    def composite_score(self) -> float:
        return round(self.rice_score * self.blast_multiplier, 1)


def prioritize_debt(items: list[DebtNode]) -> list[DebtNode]:
    """
    Sort debt items by composite score descending.
    Items that block other high-priority items are escalated.
    """
    # First pass: sort by composite score
    sorted_items = sorted(items, key=lambda x: x.composite_score, reverse=True)

    # Second pass: escalation — if a debt item blocks a top-5 item, elevate it
    top_ids = {item.id for item in sorted_items[:5]}
    escalated = []
    rest = []
    for item in sorted_items:
        if any(blocked_id in top_ids for blocked_id in item.blocks):
            escalated.append(item)
        else:
            rest.append(item)

    return escalated + rest


# Example debt register
debt_register = [
    DebtNode(
        id="DEBT-002",
        title="Pinned model version (no upgrade path)",
        category="model",
        reach=5000,
        impact=3.0,       # massive — total outage on deprecation
        confidence=1.0,   # high — deprecation date announced
        effort=8,
        dependents=["feature:summarize", "feature:chat", "feature:search", "DEBT-003"],
        blocks=["DEBT-007"],  # eval suite needed first
    ),
    DebtNode(
        id="DEBT-005",
        title="Synchronous tool calls blocking main thread",
        category="integration",
        reach=1200,
        impact=2.0,
        confidence=0.8,
        effort=5,
        dependents=["feature:search", "feature:summarize", "feature:agent"],
        blocks=[],
    ),
    DebtNode(
        id="DEBT-009",
        title="Token count not logged",
        category="observability",
        reach=5000,
        impact=0.5,       # low — no user-visible impact, but blocks cost optimization
        confidence=0.8,
        effort=2,
        dependents=["cost-optimization-project"],
        blocks=[],
    ),
    DebtNode(
        id="DEBT-007",
        title="No eval suite for model upgrade",
        category="model",
        reach=5000,
        impact=2.0,
        confidence=1.0,
        effort=13,
        dependents=["DEBT-002"],  # DEBT-002 can't be resolved without this
        blocks=[],
    ),
]

prioritized = prioritize_debt(debt_register)

print(f"{'ID':<12} {'RICE':>7} {'Blast':>8} {'Composite':>10} {'Title'}")
print("-" * 80)
for item in prioritized:
    print(f"{item.id:<12} {item.rice_score:>7.0f} {item.blast_radius_level:>8} "
          f"{item.composite_score:>10.0f}  {item.title}")
Composite Score Formula
The composite score combines RICE with the blast radius multiplier:
Composite = (Reach × Impact × Confidence ÷ Effort) × Blast Radius Multiplier
This formula ensures that a debt item with moderate RICE score but critical blast radius (e.g., it blocks all AI features) ranks above a high-RICE item that affects only one isolated feature.
Worked Examples: Scoring 3 Debt Items End-to-End

## Debt Item Scoring Worksheet

### DEBT-002: Pinned model version with no upgrade eval suite
**Category:** Model debt

**RICE inputs:**
- Reach: 5,000 (MAU of all AI features, which share this model config)
- Impact: 3.0 ("massive" — on deprecation day, all AI features go offline)
- Confidence: 100% (deprecation date officially announced by provider)
- Effort: 8 story points (upgrade + config change + validation run)

**RICE score:** (5,000 × 3.0 × 1.0) ÷ 8 = **1,875**

**Blast radius:**
- Dependents: feature:summarize, feature:chat, feature:search, DEBT-003 (blocked by this)
- Level: Critical (4 downstream nodes) → Multiplier: 5

**Composite score:** 1,875 × 5 = **9,375** → Priority: P0
**Sprint recommendation:** This sprint, immediately. Add DEBT-007 (eval suite) as prerequisite.

---

### DEBT-005: Synchronous tool calls blocking main thread
**Category:** Integration debt

**RICE inputs:**
- Reach: 1,200 (MAU of features using the affected tool client)
- Impact: 2.0 ("high" — tool timeouts cause 3% of agent turns to fail)
- Confidence: 80% (observed in production but not at full load)
- Effort: 5 story points

**RICE score:** (1,200 × 2.0 × 0.8) ÷ 5 = **384**

**Blast radius:**
- Dependents: feature:search, feature:summarize, feature:agent
- Level: High (3 nodes) → Multiplier: 4

**Composite score:** 384 × 4 = **1,536** → Priority: P1
**Sprint recommendation:** Sprint N+1 after DEBT-002 is resolved.

---

### DEBT-009: Token count not logged
**Category:** Observability debt

**RICE inputs:**
- Reach: 5,000 (affects all AI feature users indirectly through cost)
- Impact: 0.5 ("low" — no direct user-visible impact; blocks cost optimization)
- Confidence: 80% (confirmed gap via observability audit)
- Effort: 2 story points (quick win)

**RICE score:** (5,000 × 0.5 × 0.8) ÷ 2 = **1,000**

**Blast radius:**
- Dependents: cost-optimization-project (1 downstream project)
- Level: Medium (1 node) → Multiplier: 2

**Composite score:** 1,000 × 2 = **2,000** → Priority: P1 (quick win)
**Sprint recommendation:** Bundle into DEBT-002 sprint as a 2-point add-on.
Re-scoring Cadence

Composite scores are not permanent — they become stale as the product grows, the team learns more, and the external environment changes (model deprecations, provider outages, new features). Establish a regular rescore cadence:
Monthly rescore of top-10 items:
update Reach based on current MAU (which grows over time), revise Confidence as more production data becomes available, and update blast radius if new features depend on the debt item.
Trigger-based rescore:

immediately rescore when a model deprecation is announced (elevates all model-debt items), when a new feature is added that depends on an existing debt item (elevates blast radius), or when an incident is traced back to a known debt item (elevates Confidence to 100%).
Quarterly full rescore:

re-evaluate all open items in the register. Items that have been open for 90+ days with no status change may have been resolved opportunistically (close them) or may have become permanently deferred (drop them with a rationale).
Escalation Criteria
Certain conditions escalate a debt item to the top of the sprint queue regardless of its computed composite score:
Blocks a planned feature:
if a debt item blocks a feature that is in the current sprint's scope, it is automatically P0 for this sprint regardless of composite score.
Hard external deadline within 60 days:
model deprecation dates or contract renewal deadlines create hard cutoffs. Items within 60 days of a hard deadline escalate to P0 regardless of composite score.
Observed security failure:
any debt item where an actual security incident (even a minor one) has been traced to the debt bypasses the scoring system entirely and goes into the current sprint.
P0 support incident in the last sprint:
if a debt item caused a P0 incident, it escalates to the top of the next sprint's debt allocation regardless of other priorities.
When to Use This Framework
When the debt register has more than 10 open items and the team cannot resolve all of them in the next PI. The framework provides a principled ordering for the items that do get allocated capacity.

When stakeholders challenge the team's debt prioritization decisions. A composite score backed by explicit inputs (Reach from MAU data, Confidence from incident history) is far more defensible than "the engineers said this one was important."
When the team has multiple architecture-tier debt items that cannot all be accommodated in one PI. The composite score determines which architectural items are in the next PI vs. the one after.
Common Mistakes
Using severity score instead of composite score:

teams that sort by severity alone miss the blast radius multiplier. A severity-8 item with blast radius "critical" (5× multiplier) outranks a severity-12 item with blast radius "low" (1×). Composite = 40 vs 12.
Overconfidence in Confidence scores:
teams often use 80% confidence for items where there is no production data to support the impact estimate. Use 50% unless you have actual production evidence.
Not updating the dependency graph:

new features are added every sprint. If the dependency graph is not updated when a new feature depends on an existing debt item, the blast radius score is understated and the item is deprioritized incorrectly.
Ignoring the escalation criteria:

teams that apply the composite score rigidly without the escalation criteria will deprioritize a feature-blocking debt item because its composite score is lower than a high-blast-radius observability item. Escalation criteria exist for exactly this case.
Best Practices
Use real MAU data for Reach. Round to the nearest 100 for sprint planning; exact precision is not required and creates false confidence in the score.
Cap Impact at 2.0 for items that degrade quality but don't cause outages. Reserve 3.0 for items where the failure mode is total unavailability of the feature.

Automate the composite score calculation. A simple Python script that reads the YAML register and outputs a sorted priority list removes the temptation to adjust scores manually to prefer certain items.
Present the top-5 items from the composite-sorted list at each sprint planning meeting. The team reviews only these 5 for the current sprint's debt allocation — not the full register.
Performance and Scalability

As MAU grows, Reach scores for all debt items grow proportionally. A debt item that was low-Reach at 500 MAU becomes high-Reach at 50,000 MAU without any change to the debt itself. This is why monthly rescoring of Reach is mandatory.

For teams with multiple products (different AI features at different maturity levels), maintain separate debt registers per product but share a unified composite-score view for the PI planning session. Cross-product debt items (shared infrastructure) are scored against the aggregate Reach of all products.
Tooling and Ecosystem
Scoring computation:
a Python script reading the YAML register (as shown above) is the simplest implementation. A Notion formula or Airtable rollup formula can automate scoring if the register is in those tools.
Dependency visualization:
Mermaid.js for small registers (export from YAML to Mermaid diagram); Graphviz or D3.js for larger registers. Include the dependency graph in the quarterly debt review presentation.
MAU data sourcing:
pull Reach values from your analytics system (Amplitude, Mixpanel, or product database) at the time of each monthly rescore. Do not use estimates from memory.
Comparison with Alternatives
Prioritization Method
Pros
Cons
RICE + Blast Radius (this approach)
Defensible, data-driven, accounts for downstream impact
Requires MAU data and dependency mapping; scoring takes ~30 min per item
Severity score only (impact × blast)
Simple, fast
Misses effort normalization and Reach — a 1-point quick win may rank far lower than it should
Team vote (planning poker)
Fast, builds consensus
Not defensible to stakeholders; biased toward items team members find interesting
Engineering manager decides
Fast, no ceremony
Single point of view; does not leverage team's distributed knowledge of blast radius
Security and Reliability

Security debt items should have their Confidence set to 100% regardless of evidence level — because the impact of a security failure is not a degradation but a breach. Treat all security debt as confirmed high-impact.

When evaluating blast radius for a shared infrastructure component (e.g., a common LLM client library), count not just the features that use it today but also the features that will be added in the next PI. Use the roadmap as an input to blast radius estimation.
Mental Model
Think of the composite score as a
structural engineering risk assessment

. A crack in a wall (severity) is evaluated differently depending on whether it is in a load-bearing wall (blast radius: critical) or a partition wall (blast radius: low). The crack in the partition wall might be larger but less urgent to fix. The crack in the load-bearing wall is urgent because it threatens the entire structure. RICE scores the crack size and visibility; blast radius determines whether it is in a load-bearing wall. Composite score is the final risk rating that determines the repair schedule.
Key Takeaways
RICE scoring (Reach × Impact × Confidence ÷ Effort) measures the business cost of leaving a debt item unresolved, normalized by the effort to resolve it.
Blast radius multiplies the RICE score by 1–5× based on how many downstream features, debt items, or teams are affected if the debt item causes a failure.
Dependency mapping identifies which debt items must be resolved before others can be resolved — critical for scheduling multi-sprint debt reduction work.
Rescore monthly for Reach (which grows with MAU) and when trigger events occur (model deprecation announcement, new feature depending on a debt item, incident traced to debt).
Escalation criteria override the composite score when a debt item blocks a planned feature, has a hard deadline within 60 days, or caused a recent security or P0 incident.

Automate the composite score calculation. A script that reads the YAML register and outputs a sorted priority list removes scoring subjectivity and makes the prioritization defensible to stakeholders.

Quick wins (low Effort, high Confidence) often punch above their weight in composite score. Bundle them with higher-effort items in the same sprint to maintain team momentum during debt reduction periods.
